cataloging_subject.label: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The two-work edited composite represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00071: Wilhelm Heuser's 1907 presentation of the complete Middle English St Katherine hymn attributed through an acrostic to Richard Spaldyng and the distinct anonymous Hymn on the Five Joys of Mary added later to the same parchment roll. This record does not merge their authorship, hands, forms of acrostic, physical zones, or localization histories, and it does not treat Heuser's omitted German article study, the Bodleian roll, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML as the same intellectual object.

catalog_summary.title: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle
catalog_summary.author: Richard Spaldyng (attributed) and an anonymous poet
catalog_summary.date: Both works are attested in a roll dated 1400–1410; their exact composition dates are unresolved; first printed in 1907
catalog_summary.region: England, with south Lincolnshire authorial and scribal dialect associations; the exact composition places and the meaning of PIPWEL remain unresolved

titles.value: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle
The St Katherine Hymn of Richard Spaldyng and a Marian Hymn from the Same Parchment Roll
notes: Discovery translation of Heuser's German title; it is not presented as a historical title.
Heuser's printed heading, followed by the byline 'von Ricardus Spaldyng.'

titles.value: Katereyn, þe curteys of alle þat I know
Hymne auf die fünf Freuden der Maria
Poem on the Five Joys of the Virgin

notes: The XML prints the subheading 'Scā Maria'; DIMEV records Sancta Maria as an attributed title at the start.

titles.value: Myldyste of moode & mekyst of maydyns alle

agents.name: Richard Spaldyng
notes: Name supplied by an acrostic in the Katherine hymn; the precise role identified by the acrostic remains unresolved.

agents.name: Anonymous poet of the Five Joys hymn

notes: Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. Heuser, the current Bodleian catalog, and DIMEV interpret the name as authorial, but the acrostic alone does not eliminate other historical roles.
Heuser separately argues that the surviving copy is not the original and that its calligraphic scribe is unlikely to be the composer; author and witness scribe are not conflated.
The MARIA and PIPWEL acrostics do not name a person. The later hand is a witness-level fact and is not treated as proof of a distinct author-scribe identity.
Heuser prints both poems directly from MS. Bodl. Rolls 22 and states his normalization and abbreviation-expansion practices.

scope.description: Footnotes, supplied readings, and immediate editorial headings retained in CME00071

date_statements.display: Both poems are attested in MS. Bodl. Rolls 22, dated 1400–1410; the Five Joys poem was added in a later hand within the witness's production history
notes: This dates the surviving roll and supplies attestation, not exact composition dates for either poem.
The relative sequence of hands does not justify inventing a distinct date range for the Five Joys composition.

date_statements.display: Heuser first printed both poems in Anglia volume 30 in 1907
notes: Heuser explicitly describes the two hymns as printed here for the first time.

scope.description: Modern scholarly analysis of authorial and scribal linguistic relicts

notes: The current Bodleian catalog summarizes Ruth Kennedy's argument for south Lincolnshire authorial and scribal relicts. This is a dialect association, not a confirmed place of composition for both poems.

scope.description: Possible referent of the PIPWEL acrostic

notes: The current catalog reports Kennedy's proposal that PIPWEL refers to Pipwell Manor near Holbeach; the acrostic's exact referent is unresolved.

scope.description: Older possible provenance inferred from the PIPWEL acrostic

notes: Heuser and MLGB3 connect PIPWEL with the Cistercian abbey at Pipewell, Northamptonshire, but the current catalog preserves Kennedy's competing Lincolnshire interpretation. Neither is cataloged as a confirmed composition place.

scope.description: Heuser's editorial dateline at the end of the printed composite

notes: Göttingen is Heuser's editorial place, not a medieval place of composition or copying.

language_statements.label: German

scope.description: Heuser's whole title, part headings, byline, footnotes, and editorial closing context

notes: German is editorial language around the two Middle English poems; it is not attributed to either medieval poet.

language_statements.label: Latin
notes: A short heading does not make the medieval poem substantively bilingual.

form_statements.description: Two complete Middle English poems. German headings, footnotes, and the closing dateline are editorial paratext rather than substantive prose parts of the encoded composite.

subject_statements.label: Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion
Five Joys of the Virgin Mary
notes: The five stanzas praise the Annunciation, Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Assumption, and Coronation, followed by a six-line prayer.

summaries.value: The first poem is a twenty-stanza alliterative hymn to Saint Katherine of Alexandria. It recounts her disputation with fifty rhetoricians, their conversion and martyrdom, the conversions and deaths of the empress and Porphyry, Katherine's torture and beheading, the translation of her body to Sinai, and her power to intercede, before closing in direct prayer. Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. The second and formally independent poem praises five joys of Mary—the Annunciation, Christ's Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Mary's Assumption, and Coronation—and ends by asking her intercession. Its stanza initials spell MARIA and the six-line envoy spells PIPWEL.

CME00071 begins on Heuser's printed p. 539 with the St Katherine text, crosses shared p. 547 from the end of that poem into the Five Joys hymn, and ends with Heuser's signature on p. 548. It omits the German article title and study on pp. 523–538. In the physical roll, the first poem occupies the face and most of the dorse; the second is a later-hand addition at the foot of the dorse. The sole public image is only a cropped top detail of the opening St Katherine stanzas.

extent.description: Two top-level XML poem divisions. The St Katherine hymn contains 20 numbered LG elements and exactly 280 L elements; the Five Joys hymn contains five numbered eight-line LG elements plus one unnumbered six-line envoy, totaling 46 L elements. The complete XML has 26 LG, 326 L, 31 NOTE, and 10 PB elements. These are encoding metrics aligned with the institutional and DIMEV stanza descriptions, not a claim about every possible critical lineation.

content_parts.label: St. Katharina / Alliterative Katherine Hymn
content_parts.summary: A twenty-stanza, 280-line Middle English saints' hymn combining Katherine's legend with praise and intercessory prayer; stanzas 17–19 carry the KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG acrostic.

locators.value: top of roll; complete face and most of dorse
one canvas labeled top; stanzas 1–2 and opening of stanza 3 visible

notes: The poem ends on shared printed p. 547 above the start of the Five Joys hymn.
The public image is mapped only to its actual opening overlap and is not treated as a complete facsimile.

content_parts.label: Hymn on the Five Joys of Mary
content_parts.summary: An anonymous 46-line Marian hymn in five eight-line stanzas whose initials spell MARIA, followed by a six-line intercessory envoy spelling PIPWEL.

locators.value: foot of roll, in remaining space on the dorse, in a later hand

notes: The poem begins on shared printed p. 547 below the close of the Katherine hymn.
The one public Digital Bodleian crop has zero overlap with this poem; no facsimile locator is invented.
The witness's later hand is not treated as evidence for a named author or a separately normalized composition date.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME00071 source XML and structural inspection
evidence.locator: IDG; REVDESCR; TEXT/@LANG; two DIV1 units; PB N=539–548; 26 LG; 326 L; 31 NOTE; HEAD, BYLINE, HI, GAP, CLOSER, DATELINE, and SIGNED
evidence.summary: Verifies identifiers, title and part headings, complete two-poem boundary, stanza and line counts, encoded Middle English content, German and Latin paratext, acrostic typography, source pages, Heuser's notes and expansions, final dateline, and the 2016 revision statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00071 and About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: Confirms the stable U-M item identity and public access; the collection describes manual keying from public-domain editions, in-house proofing and editing, and basic TEI markup.

evidence.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, 'Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle,' Anglia 30 (1907), 523–548
evidence.locator: roll description pp. 523–525; editorial preliminaries pp. 538–539; Katherine pp. 539–547; Five Joys pp. 547–548
evidence.summary: Direct inspection establishes bibliographic identity, first-publication claim, the two distinct poems and their printed boundaries, the face/dorse physical mapping, different hands and later addition, acrostic and localization arguments, subject matter, stanza forms, editorial normalization and expansions, and the Göttingen signature.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Bodl. Rolls 22
evidence.locator: contents items 1–2; physical description; hands; history; surrogates; revision 2025-03-20
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact shelfmark, two component identities, incipits and explicits, stanza and acrostic structures, Middle English language, roll form and dimensions, early-fifteenth-century date, separate hands and later Five Joys addition, south Lincolnshire linguistic argument, competing PIPWEL interpretations, and one-image partial surrogate.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, DIMEV 2983
evidence.summary: Confirms the St Katherine hymn's identity, Richard Spaldyng attribution, twenty 14-line acrostic stanzas, rhyme scheme, sole roll witness, incipit and explicit, and Heuser text pages 539–547.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, DIMEV 3498
evidence.summary: Confirms the anonymous Five Joys hymn's identity, five eight-line MARIA stanzas and six-line PIPWEL envoy, rhyme scheme, Sancta Maria attributed title, sole roll witness, and incipit and explicit. Direct scan inspection corrects the record's erroneous subordinate Heuser text-page field to pp. 547–548.

Provides entity, relation, page and roll-surface mapping, whole-source cache, partial-image boundary, access, rights, editorial-practice, and unresolved-question evidence for this work-metadata record.

open_questions.question: Does the KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG acrostic securely identify the Katherine hymn's author, or could it identify another participant?
next_steps: Review Ruth Kennedy's complete critical edition and guild study and compare their authorial, scribal, and social-context arguments against a complete facsimile; retain the attributed-author label meanwhile.

open_questions.question: What are the defensible composition dates and places of the two poems, and what does PIPWEL identify?
next_steps: Review Kennedy's full linguistic and historical analyses and a complete roll facsimile; keep witness production in 1400–1410 separate from composition and preserve Pipewell Abbey and Pipwell Manor as competing associations.

open_questions.question: Can a complete whole-roll facsimile be obtained and used for exact physical and typographic research?
next_steps: Request both face and dorse, every membrane, end, join, tab or attachment, and rolled and unrolled views from Bodleian copying or Imaging Services; map both poem zones separately and obtain explicit reuse terms.
notes: The current public object is only one top-of-roll crop and has no Five Joys overlap.

open_questions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains omission of pp. 523–538 and removal of an introductory German fragment in 2016?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for source-selection and revision documentation while preserving the directly observed XML-to-print mapping.

notes: The preferred whole-record title is Heuser's exact German composite title; the English title is a discovery translation.
The two medieval poems are modeled as independent work parts sharing one roll witness and one Heuser edition, not as one medieval author's oeuvre.
The later Five Joys hand is a physical-witness fact. It is not converted into named authorship, a precise composition date, or a separate physical object.
The public top crop is bound only to the St Katherine part and only to the stanzas actually visible; the Five Joys part has no public image mapping.
German editorial paratext and one Latin subheading are recorded without changing the whole expression's verse form.
